name: Sulfur
notes: On peak evenings the hatch is dense — go small and exact. South Holston is the standout.

name: Blue-Winged Olive (spring)
searchTerm: blue winged olive BWO Baetis mayfly
notes: Best on overcast / drizzly days. Multi-brooded so they show up multiple times a year.

name: Blue-Winged Olive (fall)
searchTerm: blue winged olive BWO Baetis mayfly
notes: Fall return; even smaller. Trout key on emergers.

name: Hendrickson
notes: First major mayfly hatch of the year up north. Kicks off dry-fly season.

name: March Brown
notes: Sporadic emergence rather than dense hatches. Big bite when it comes off.

name: Grannom Caddis
notes: Mother's-Day caddis on many rivers. Bites both surface and subsurface — fish a dropper.

name: Cinnamon Caddis
notes: Reliable evening hatch all summer long.

name: Yellow Sally Stonefly
notes: Small bright-yellow stonefly. Fish like them as a searching pattern even when no rises.

name: Isonychia
notes: Swim-fast nymphs trout key on. Great pre-hatch swing pattern.

name: Trico
notes: Spinner fall first thing in the morning. Fish 6x or 7x tippet.

name: Hexagenia (the Hex)
notes: The hatch. Night fishing, big browns. Plan around it. Typical peak: third week of June on the Manistee.

name: White Mayfly
notes: Dense late-summer evening hatches; pure-white wings make it unmistakable.

name: Midges
notes: Year-round; especially the only game in winter. Trail behind a heavier nymph.

name: Terrestrials (ants, beetles, hoppers)
searchTerm: terrestrial fly pattern hopper ant beetle
notes: Summer bread-and-butter when no hatch is happening. Plop them under overhanging banks.

name: Salmonfly (Western)
notes: The biggest mayfly hatch in the West — the 'salmonfly' is actually a stonefly, 3 in long. Hatch moves upstream like a wave; trout key in for 7-10 days. Big Hole + Madison + Yellowstone are the legendary salmonfly waters.

name: PMD (Western)
searchTerm: pale morning dun PMD Ephemerella excrucians
notes: Pale Morning Dun — the iconic Western mayfly. Henry's Fork PMD blanket hatch is bucket-list. Mornings, fish slowly on long leaders + 6X tippet.

name: Golden Stone (Western)
notes: Trails the salmonfly hatch by ~2 weeks and overlaps with PMDs. Big dry-fly opportunity all summer; nymphs work year-round.

name: Callibaetis (Western stillwater)
notes: Western stillwater mayfly. Mornings on calm reservoirs and spring creeks. Henry's Lake + Hebgen + Strawberry are textbook callibaetis water.

name: Skwala Stonefly (Western)
notes: Early-season Western hatch — late March / early April on the Bitterroot is the signature. Smaller olive cousin of salmonfly; trout get drowned-out aggressive on the first warm afternoons.

name: Mahogany Dun (Western)
notes: Fall Western mayfly — overlaps with BWOs. Dark cinnamon body; trout key on them on overcast Sept/Oct afternoons.

name: Green Drake (Western)
notes: Henry's Fork green drake hatch is one of fly fishing's iconic events. Big mayfly — trout move for it. Late June into July, afternoons.

name: October Caddis (Western)
notes: Last big dry-fly opportunity of the year. Late-September into mid-October — fall colors + big orange caddis on the surface. Big browns key on them before the spawn.

name: Trico (Western tailwaters)
notes: Tailwater trico hatch — Missouri below Holter, Bighorn below Afterbay. Morning spinner falls; 7X tippet, picky fish. Same insect as Eastern trico, more reliable on Western tailwaters.

name: Quill Gordon
notes: The first mayfly hatch of the year in the Smokies. Cold, often drizzly early-spring afternoons. Trout aren't selective yet — close-enough patterns work.

name: Light Cahill
notes: Late-evening hatch through warm summer months. Pale cream body + light wings — easy to spot in low light. Spinner falls right at dark.

name: Slate Drake
notes: Late-summer / fall counterpart to Isonychia bicolor. Crawls out on rocks to emerge — fish wets and emergers near banks at dusk.

name: Sulfur (MI rivers)
notes: Mid-May through June on the Au Sable + Manistee. Smaller than the South Holston version. Evening rises through twilight.

name: Brown Drake
notes: Late-June dusk hatch — Hex precursor on MI rivers; Henrys Fork bucket-list event. Spinner fall at last light is THE event. Window is narrow (~10 days).

name: Mother's Day Caddis
notes: Yellowstone + Madison legend — dense swarms in late April / early May right before runoff. Fish lose their minds. Often coincides with the holiday weekend.

name: Spruce Moth
notes: Late summer terrestrial — adults blown into the water from spruce/fir stands. Fish key on them HARD on the Madison + upper Yellowstone. Morning is best; calm water.

name: Pale Evening Dun (PED)
searchTerm: pale evening dun Ephemerella inermis PED
notes: Evening cousin of the PMD — pale yellow body, smaller. Henrys Fork + Bighorn evening fishing through summer. Trout get picky as light fades — go small.

name: Flav (Small Western Green Drake)
notes: Smaller follow-up to the Western Green Drake. July-August afternoons. Olive body, mottled wings — important on the Madison + Henrys Fork.

name: Pink Albert
notes: Henrys Fork specialty — pinkish body, evening rise on flat water. The picky-fish hatch. 7X tippet, drag-free or nothing.

name: Hydropsyche / Spotted Sedge
notes: Most-common summer caddis across both Western + Eastern tailwaters. Larva (green rock-worm) is a year-round nymph go-to. Adults fall hard in evening.

name: Little Yellow Stone (Western)
notes: Western counterpart to Yellow Sally. Summer afternoon hatch — fish all day on most Western freestone. Often blanket-hatch on the Madison.

name: Mysis Shrimp (tailwater staple)
notes: Trophy-fish food on the Frying Pan (Toilet Bowl), Bighorn, and Green. Drift through the dam outflow — that's where the protein is. Mysis are 0.5-1 in long, translucent white.

name: Scud (tailwater staple)
notes: Year-round protein staple on tailwaters. Dead-drift on the bottom — split-shot, indicator. Pink scuds work where rainbows have seen everything.

name: Sowbug / Cress Bug
searchTerm: sowbug cress bug fly pattern tailwater
notes: Year-round tailwater protein, especially Arkansas + Pennsylvania limestone. Sister-fly to scud but flatter, drabber. Bounce them along bottom seams.

name: San Juan Worm (universal nymph)
flies: Bead-Head Green Weenie — PA-born inchworm/caterpillar suggestion; deadly on the Little Juniata
searchTerm: san juan worm squirmy worm fly pattern
notes: Cheating? Maybe. Catches fish? Always. Fish it on a tight-line under an indicator anywhere worms wash in — spring runoff, post-rain, tailwaters.

name: King Salmon Eggs (MI fall run)
flies: Size 8 orange / pink egg cluster (skein imitation)
searchTerm: king salmon eggs spawn fly Michigan
notes: September-October kings stack below dams (Tippy, Berrien Springs). Dead-drift egg patterns + flesh flies behind the redds. Steelhead + browns follow the eggs.

name: Steelhead Eggs + Beads (MI winter / spring run)
flies: Size 8-10 single egg (oregon cheese, salmon roe, peach)
Sexy Hex Nymph — searching nymph between drifts
searchTerm: steelhead eggs beads fly Great Lakes
notes: Fall steelhead chase king eggs Oct-Nov; winter holdovers continue through April. Beads on bead-pegs above a bare hook is the modern center-pin / fly-rod standard.

name: Articulated Streamers (MI trophy browns)
flies: Butt Monkey (Galloup) — dark colors at night
Chuck Hawkins's Parr None — early-spring salmon-parr imitation
Size 4-8 olive / black / white Wooly Bugger
Size 4 weighted Wet Skunk — work tight to wood
searchTerm: articulated streamer fly brown trout Michigan
notes: Fall pre-spawn brown-trout play. Heavy sink-tip, swing through holes. Lower light + falling temps + recent rain = streamer prime time. Strip-pause-strip.

name: Tiny Black Stonefly (MI)
flies: Size 16-20 Tiny Black Stonefly nymph (Pat's rubber-legs)
notes: Michigan-specific hatch. 12:00-16:00

name: Early Black Stonefly (MI)
flies: Size 10-14 Early Black Stonefly nymph (Pat's rubber-legs)
notes: Michigan-specific hatch. 12:00-16:00

name: Early Brown Stonefly (MI)
flies: Size 12-14 Early Brown Stonefly nymph (Pat's rubber-legs)
notes: Michigan-specific hatch. 13:00-19:00

flies: Size 16-18 Slate Wing Mahogany CDC emerger
Size 16-18 Slate Wing Mahogany rusty spinner
notes: Michigan-specific hatch. Peak 5/14-5/28. 10:00-16:00

name: Little Black Caddis (MI)
notes: Michigan-specific hatch. Peak 5/14-5/21. 12:00-22:00

name: Borcher's Drake (MI)
notes: Michigan-specific hatch. 10:00-16:00

name: Green Caddis / Green Rock Worm (MI)
notes: Michigan-specific hatch. 14:00-Dark

flies: Size 8-14 Medium Brown Stonefly nymph (Pat's rubber-legs)
notes: Michigan-specific hatch. Afternoons

notes: Michigan-specific hatch. 12:00-17:00

name: Giant Stonefly / Salmonfly (MI)
notes: Michigan-specific hatch. Anytime

flies: Size 10-14 Great Speckled Olive Mayfly parachute
Size 10-14 Great Speckled Olive Mayfly Compara-dun
Size 10-14 Great Speckled Olive Mayfly cripple

Size 10-14 Great Speckled Olive Mayfly rusty spinner

name: True Hendrickson (MI)
notes: Michigan-specific hatch. Peak 6/4-6/21. 12:00-17:00

name: Blue-Winged Olive (evening) (MI)
notes: Michigan-specific hatch. 20:00-22:00

name: Tiny Blue-Winged Olive (MI)
flies: Size 20-22 Tiny Blue-Winged Olive CDC emerger
Size 20-22 Tiny Blue-Winged Olive rusty spinner
notes: Michigan-specific hatch. 10:00-21:00

name: Green Oak Worm (MI)
notes: Michigan-specific hatch. Peak 6/21-7/5. Daytime

name: White Miller Caddis (MI)
notes: Michigan-specific hatch. Afternoons

name: Early Gray Drake (MI)
flies: Size 12-14 Early Gray Drake CDC emerger
Size 12-14 Early Gray Drake rusty spinner
notes: Michigan-specific hatch. Peak 6/14-6/21. Dark

name: Sand Drake (MI)
notes: Michigan-specific hatch. Peak 6/14-6/28. 20:00-24:00

name: Quill Gordon (MI)
notes: Michigan-specific hatch. 13:00-16:00

name: Pale Evening Dun (MI)
flies: Size 16-18 Pale Evening Dun CDC emerger
Size 16-18 Pale Evening Dun rusty spinner
notes: Michigan-specific hatch. 20:00-24:00

name: Great Orange Sedge (MI)
notes: Michigan-specific hatch. Evenings

name: Beetles (MI)
notes: Michigan-specific hatch. Daytime & Evenings

name: Late Gray Drake (MI)
flies: Size 10-12 Late Gray Drake CDC emerger
Size 10-12 Late Gray Drake rusty spinner
notes: Michigan-specific hatch. Peak 7/15-7/28. Dark

name: Mahogany Dun (MI)
notes: Michigan-specific hatch. 16:00-22:00

name: Golden Drake (MI)
notes: Michigan-specific hatch.

name: Leadwing Coachman / Iso (MI)
notes: Michigan-specific hatch. 16:00-22:00

name: Big Golden Stonefly (MI)
flies: Size 6-8 Big Golden Stonefly nymph (Pat's rubber-legs)
notes: Michigan-specific hatch.

name: Slate Winged Olive (MI)
flies: Size 14-18 Slate Winged Olive CDC emerger
Size 14-18 Slate Winged Olive rusty spinner
notes: Michigan-specific hatch.

flies: Size 18-20 Dark Slate Wing Olive parachute
Size 18-20 Dark Slate Wing Olive Compara-dun
Size 18-20 Dark Slate Wing Olive cripple
Size 18-20 Dark Slate Wing Olive CDC emerger
Size 18-20 Dark Slate Wing Olive rusty spinner
notes: Michigan-specific hatch.

flies: Size 22-24 Tiny Blue Winged Olive parachute
Size 22-24 Tiny Blue Winged Olive Compara-dun
Size 22-24 Tiny Blue Winged Olive cripple
Size 22-24 Tiny Blue Winged Olive CDC emerger
Size 22-24 Tiny Blue Winged Olive rusty spinner
notes: Michigan-specific hatch.

name: Crickets (MI)
notes: Michigan-specific hatch. Anytime

name: Grasshoppers (MI)
flies: Joe's Hopper aka Michigan Hopper (Art Winnie original, Traverse City — genuine MI classic, 1900s)
Madsen's Skunk — MI classic; fish wet or dry
notes: Michigan-specific hatch. Daytime

name: Small Slate Mahogany (MI)
flies: Size 16-18 Small Slate Mahogany CDC emerger
Size 16-18 Small Slate Mahogany rusty spinner
notes: Michigan-specific hatch.

notes: Michigan-specific hatch.

flies: Size 22-24 Little Medium Olive CDC emerger
Size 22-24 Little Medium Olive rusty spinner

flies: Size 14-16 Slate Wing Brown Mayfly parachute
Size 14-16 Slate Wing Brown Mayfly Compara-dun
Size 14-16 Slate Wing Brown Mayfly cripple
Size 14-16 Slate Wing Brown Mayfly CDC emerger
Size 14-16 Slate Wing Brown Mayfly rusty spinner
notes: Michigan-specific hatch.

name: October Caddis (MI)
notes: Michigan-specific hatch. Afternoons/Evenings

name: Tiny Winter BWO
flies: Size 18-22 Tiny Winter BWO CDC emerger
Size 18-22 Tiny Winter BWO rusty spinner
notes: Peak: Feb-Mar overcast days. Best waters: Tailwaters and Smokies. Cloudy/drizzly afternoons are best

name: Early Black Stonefly
flies: Size 16-18 Early Black Stonefly nymph (Pat's rubber-legs)
notes: Peak: Feb-Mar. Best waters: Smokies freestones and tailwaters. Hookers Fly Shop lists in January

name: Little Black Caddis
notes: Peak: Mid-March-mid-April. Best waters: South Holston and Watauga. Strong Watauga hatch 3/15-4/15

name: Blue Quill
notes: Peak: April. Best waters: Smokies and freestones. Often overlaps with Quill Gordon

name: Giant Black Stonefly
flies: Size 4-8 Giant Black Stonefly nymph (Pat's rubber-legs)
notes: Peak: May. Best waters: Smokies. Evening emergence on warm days

name: Eastern Green Drake
flies: Size 8-12 Eastern Green Drake CDC emerger
Size 8-12 Eastern Green Drake rusty spinner
notes: Peak: Early-mid May. Best waters: Smokies and Hiwassee. Biggest mayfly of the year in the park

name: Cicadas (periodic)
flies: Size 6 Chubby Chernobyl (cicada colors: black body, orange post)
notes: Peak: Cicada years only. Best waters: Cumberland Plateau and East TN. Brood years produce huge surface eats

name: Inchworms
flies: Bead-Head Green Weenie — PA-born inchworm classic
notes: Peak: Late May-June. Best waters: Smokies and freestones. Green inchworm patterns

name: Autumn Sedge / October Caddis
notes: Peak: October. Best waters: All tailwaters and freestones. Orange-bodied; extends dry fly season

name: Shad Hatch (forage event)
searchTerm: Alosa sp. forage fish fly fishing
notes: Peak: January-February. Best waters: Chattahoochee below Buford Dam. White/silver streamers when shad pass through dam

name: Yellow Drake
notes: Peak: Summer. Best waters: Toccoa and freestones.

name: Mouse Patterns (MI night game)
flies: Lynch's White-Bellied Mouse (Tommy Lynch) — trophy-brown specialist
McCoy's Mouse (Ed McCoy) — wiggle tail + foam head, deer-mouse profile
searchTerm: mouse pattern fly fishing Michigan trophy brown trout night
notes: MI's other claim to fame after Hex. Big browns hunt mice along undercut banks + log jams after dark. Tommy Lynch's White-Bellied Mouse and Ed McCoy's Mouse are MI-origin classics. Fish heavy tippet (1X-2X) — strikes are aggressive.

name: Attractor Dries (searching patterns)
flies: Patriot Fly (red/white/blue) — Josh Greenberg's go-to at Gates Au Sable Lodge
Purple Patriot — May version of the Patriot
Purple Haze — Western pattern that fishes hard in MI too
Hippie Stomper — doubles as terrestrial attractor
Madsen's Skunk — MI classic, fish wet or dry
Mr. Rapidan (Harry Murray) — small-stream brookie gold; dry-dropper rig staple
Neversink Caddis — small-stream high-floater for brookie pocket water
Parachute Adams — buggy enough to suggest a hatch; mostly searched
searchTerm: attractor dry fly Patriot Purple Haze Royal Wulff
notes: Not matching anything specific — searching patterns that prospect when nothing's coming off. Bright + visible, easy to track in riffles. Lead with these on unfamiliar water or slow afternoons.

name: Sculpins, Crayfish & Lamprey (MI subsurface protein)
flies: Articulated crayfish (orange / olive — fall spawning colors)
Chestnut Lamprey pattern — early-spring Manistee specialty
Articulated lamprey ('crawl out of mud' presentation)
searchTerm: sculpin pattern crayfish fly lamprey Michigan trout
notes: Subsurface protein anchors a MI brown's diet. Sculpins along the bottom, crayfish in slow water + the fall pre-spawn, chestnut lamprey 'crawl out of the mud' on the Manistee in early spring — trout key on them so hard some MI guides treat lamprey as a streamer match-the-hatch.

name: Night Frog Patterns (MI trophy browns + smallmouth)
flies: McCoy's Amphibious Assault (Ed McCoy) — MI guide go-to
Polar Changer — also smallmouth on lower Manistee
Diver pattern — Grand Traverse Bay smallmouth tribs
searchTerm: frog pattern fly night fishing brown trout Michigan
notes: Night fishing for trophy browns; also smallmouth on the lower Manistee and Grand Traverse Bay tribs. McCoy's Amphibious Assault is the MI guide go-to. Strip with hard pauses; strikes are explosive.

name: Letort Terrestrials (PA-invented genre)
flies: Letort Hopper (Ed Shenk + Vince Marinaro) — the original American hopper, still effective
Letort Cricket (Ed Shenk) — August limestoner go-to
Jassid (Marinaro) — leafhopper imitation, small attractor classic
Crowe Beetle — deer-hair beetle, deadly on Spring Creek + Yellow Breeches
Japanese Beetle pattern (iridescent foam) — peak July; invasive established Eastern US
Chernobyl Ant — foam attractor that fish read as generic terrestrial
Foam Ant (winged) — late-summer hot-weather hatch substitute
Flying Ant pattern — late-summer flights; few flies match its productivity Aug-Sept
Charlie Boy Hopper — modern foam workhorse
Chubby Chernobyl — modern foam, doubles as stonefly indicator
Stimulator — PA attractor, doubles as hopper / stonefly
searchTerm: Letort terrestrial Marinaro Shenk Pennsylvania limestone
notes: Vince Marinaro invented the terrestrial genre on the Letort Spring Run with his 1950 book 'A Modern Dry Fly Code.' Ed Shenk's Letort Hopper + Letort Cricket are the originals — still effective. The Letort tradition is the wellspring of American terrestrial fishing.

name: Cicadas & Spotted Lanternfly (PA-specific invasives)
flies: Spotted Lanternfly pattern — foam body, white-banded wing; fish eat them like cicadas (invasive since 2014)
Cicada / lanternfly hybrid pattern — modern foam terrestrial
Foam Cicada (Brood-X colors: black body, orange wing post)
searchTerm: spotted lanternfly cicada pattern fly fishing Pennsylvania
notes: Spotted Lanternfly (since 2014) is a genuinely new PA terrestrial — they're a nuisance bug but trout eat them like cicadas. Periodic cicada broods every 13-17 years (Brood X et al) become the most important 'non-hatch' of the decade when they happen.

name: Eastern Streamers (Wooly Bugger + Clouser were PA-born)
flies: Wooly Bugger (Russell Blessing, PA 1960s — PA-BORN, now global) — olive / black / white / brown
Clouser Deep Minnow (Bob Clouser, Susquehanna PA — PA-BORN) — chartreuse-white standard
Shenk's White Streamer (Ed Shenk, Letort) — limestone-creek killer
Black Nose Dace (Art Flick) — bucktail, matches dace in cold mountain streams
Leadeye Minnow — Spring Creek favorite, dace-style tied
Galloup's Sex Dungeon — MI-born but heavily fished on PA Lehigh / Yough / Allegheny
Conehead Bugger — weighted for deeper presentations
searchTerm: wooly bugger clouser minnow streamer Pennsylvania trout
notes: Two of the most-fished streamers on earth are PA-born: the Wooly Bugger (Russell Blessing, 1960s) and the Clouser Deep Minnow (Bob Clouser on the Susquehanna). Both take everything from small mountain brookies to tarpon. Ed Shenk's Letort patterns are the PA limestone-stream gold standard.

name: Erie Steelhead Alley (PA tribs)
flies: Sucker Spawn — classic PA-Erie egg cluster
Blood Dot Egg (Jeff Blood, PA-LEGEND) — Erie Alley original
White Death (Jeff Blood, PA) — emerald shiner imitation; staple for Erie brown trout + steelhead
Size 2-6 Wooly Bugger (beefy — black, olive, white)
Kurt's Creek Minnow — emerald shiner imitation
Size 16-20 Zebra Midge — low/clear winter water
searchTerm: erie steelhead alley fly Pennsylvania Walnut Elk Creek
notes: Steelhead Alley = almost entirely non-hatch fishing. Dead-drift eggs/nymphs under an indicator in low-clear winter water. Swing streamers in fall + spring runs. Jeff Blood's Blood Dot Egg + White Death are PA legends. Brown trout + steelhead key on emerald shiner and gizzard shad in the lake itself — White Death imitates that baitfish.

name: Junk Nymphs (Green Weenie + Mop + worms)
flies: Bead-Head Green Weenie (PA-BORN) — caterpillar/inchworm suggestion; deadly on the Little Juniata
Mop Fly (chartreuse / cream / olive) — much-debated, undeniably effective
Egg pattern (Glo Bug / Estaz) — fished even outside spawn on stocked water
Soft-hackle wet (partridge + olive / hare's ear) — searching pattern when nothing is up
searchTerm: green weenie mop fly junk nymph fly fishing
notes: Junk patterns that don't match anything specific but consistently catch fish. The Green Weenie is genuinely PA-born — for whatever reason, a Bead Head Green Weenie takes fish reliably on the Little Juniata and across PA stocked + limestone water. Mop fly + worms cover high-water + dirty-water situations.
